figures.caption: Figure 2: Per-layer routing weight distributions. Layer 0 shows the widest spread (σ=0.272), consistent with domain-adaptive specialist routing. Layer 7 is bimodal (σ=0.290, 14.8% near-zero). Layer 9 never produces weights above 0.9 despite being the most critical layer. All layer means exceed 0.5 (range 0.508–0.674); the model prefers suppression over preservation on average.
Table 6: Multiple-choice benchmark accuracy [Gao et al., 2021], n=500 examples each. BoolQ is the only benchmark where the 95% CI on the difference excludes zero.

Figure 1: Standard transformer block (left) vs. routed transformer block (right). The routing mechanism (dashed box) adds three components: a shared router MLP that produces per-input weights rh,k ∈ [0, 1] from the mean-pooled sequence, learned direction vectors dh,k, and a directional suppression step that removes selected components from each head’s output. All other components are identical.
Table 3: Induction circuit analysis. Routing is essential; individual induction heads are not.
Figure 4: Direction geometry. Mean within-head angle: 75.9° (vs. 89.4° random in 128D). Effective rank: 112.9/128 (88% capacity usage). Mild superposition, no dimensional collapse.
Figure 5: Preliminary cross-scale results at 26M and 400M parameters. Parameter overhead is 3.9% at both scales. Two data points; not a scaling law.
Figure 3: Routing benefit (∆ log-prob) by token position. Decays from +1.35 at positions 0–9 to +0.19 at 100+, a direct consequence of the mean-pooling bottleneck.
